rulings.Case Title: KAI KOPP VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant City And County Of San Francisco Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, July 24, 2018, Line 13, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant City And County Of San Francisco Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment. (part 1 of 3 of tentative ruling) Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment is denied. The City failed to satisfy its summary judgment burden showing: 1) the inapplicability of the failure to warn exception to the hazardous recreational activity immunity; 2) the City's placement of the light pole and surrounding fence did not increase the risks inherent in playing baseball; and 3) the light pole and surrounding fence is not a dangerous condition. Even if the City had satisfied its summary judgment burden on these issues, there are triable disputes as to each of them. Though no case has so held, unsupervised baseball such as that played by plaintiff Kai Kopp on the day of his collision is a "hazardous recreational activity" as that phrase is defined in Government Code 831.7(b) and (b)(3). Two cases assumed without explicitly deciding that unsupervised baseball is a hazardous recreational activity (Avila v. Citrus Community College District (2006) 38 Cal. 4th 148; Acosta v. Los Angeles Unified School District (1995) 31 Cal. App. 4th 471, 477) and common knowledge about the many injuries sustained playing baseball at all levels of the game and the frequent collisions between players brings the sport within the language of both (b) and the defined phrase "body contact sports." Nor is there any question, absent an exception to the hazardous recreational activity immunity, the immunity applies to a collision that took place in foul territory during a baseball game. (Yarber v. Oakland Unified School District (1992) 4 Cal. App. 4th 1516). Fairly construed, the first amended complaint (the operative complaint for this motion) alleges facts triggering the potential application of the failure to warn exception (831.7(c)(1)(A)) to the hazardous recreational activity immunity and thus the City bears the burden on this motion to submit sufficient facts negating the applicability of that exception. (County of San Diego v. Superior Court (2015) 242 Cal. App. 4th 460, 469). The failure to warn exception applies if the City failed to warn about the causal factor (Mr. Kopp's colliding with the fence surrounding the light pole) and the causal factor is both a "known dangerous condition" and was "not reasonably assumed" by Mr. Kopp as an inherent part of playing baseball. (Id. at 470; Government Code 831.7(c)(1)(A)). None of the 31 facts in the City's separate statement, considered separately or collectively, establish that the City provided any warning about the light pole surrounded by the fence such as a "warning track or bright markings." (Dillingham Dec. 3:11). Nor did any of those 31 facts establish that the light pole surrounded by the fence was not a dangerous condition or unknown to the City. The absence of any claims or complaints received by the City about the light post surrounded by the fence does not, without more, establish that the light post and fence was not a dangerous condition or unknown to the City. (Lane v. City of Sacramento (2010) 183 Cal. App. 4th 1337, 1345-47 (reversing summary judgment in favor of a public entity because the public entity failed to satisfy its summary judgment burden that its property was not a dangerous condition)). (end of part 1 of 3) =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: KAI KOPP VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant City And County Of San Francisco Motion For Summary Judgment Defendant City And County Of San Francisco Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment. (Part 3 Of 3 Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, July 24, 2018, Line 13, Defendant City And County Of San Francisco Motion For Summary Judgment Defendant City And County Of San Francisco Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment. (Part 3 Of 3 Of Tentative Ruling) Nor is there anything in the holdings or language of either Avila or Yarber, the two cases that the City relies on most heavily, which require granting this motion. Neither case addresses the failure to warn exception or whether a particular condition of public property is a dangerous condition. The discussion in Avila about the inherent risks of playing baseball is based on facts that are (poor pun intended) far afield from this case. Yarber only addressed whether a particular sport (basketball in that case) is a hazardous recreational activity and whether the hazardous recreational activity immunity can apply to a collision that occurs outside the lines of the playing field. As stated above, this motion does not turn on either of those issues. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Kopp is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of 3 of 3) =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: KAI KOPP VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant City And County Of San Francisco Motion For Summary Judgment Defendant City And County Of San Francisco Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment. (Part 2 Of 3 Of Tentative Ruling.)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, July 24, 2018, Line 13, Defendant City And County Of San Francisco Motion For Summary Judgment Defendant City And County Of San Francisco Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment. (Part 2 Of 3 Of Tentative Ruling.) Nor do any of the City's 31 facts establish that colliding with a light post surrounded by a fence approximately 40 feet from a foul line is encompassed within the risks inherent in playing baseball. While it may be true that colliding with a perimeter fence (i.e. a fence separating foul territory from out of play territory and fair territory from home run territory) is an inherent risk of playing baseball, neither common sense nor baseball experience necessarily establishes that collision with a light pole surrounded by a fence while chasing a foul ball is inherent in the risks that a baseball player assumes when he takes the field. Case law, most notably Luna v. Vela (2008) 169 Cal. App. 4th 102 and the cases cited therein at pages109-114, establish that whether the placement of an item by a facility owner in an area where a sports participant may collide with it is a risk inherent in the sport is usually a question of fact not amenable to resolution on a summary judgment motion. For the forgoing reasons, the City has not satisfied its burden of proving the inapplicability of the failure to warn exception and, even if it did, there is a triable dispute about the applicability of that exception. The foregoing also disposes of the City's efforts to obtain summary judgment on the grounds of primary assumption of risk doctrine and its contention that the light pole and surrounding fence is not a dangerous condition. The elements of the failure to warn exception that must be disproved by the City to prevail on this motion based on the hazardous recreational activity immunity - i.e, that the placement of the light pole surrounded by the fence is part of the inherent risks of playing baseball and the light pole and surrounded by the fence is not a dangerous condition - are identical to what the City is required to and has not disproven to prevail on its primary assumption of risk and no dangerous condition arguments. (Luna, supra (to prevail on the primary assumption of risk doctrine, a facility owner must show that its conduct did not increase the risks inherent in the sport played by the plaintiff); Government Code 830(a) (definition of "dangerous condition" applies to "this chapter" which includes both the failure to warn exception in subsection 831.7(c)(1)(A) and the claim for dangerous condition of public property in section 835); County of San Diego, supra (relying on dangerous condition of public property cases in deciding applicability of failure to warn exception)). The City's one-sentence argument that it is not liable because the light pole and surrounding fence was "obvious" begs the question of obvious to whom and from what vantage point. Surely the pole and fence are apparent to anyone walking or standing by them. But that was not Mr. Kopp's vantage point. As the City acknowledges in its Facts 11 and 12, Mr. Kopp's focus was on the foul ball (which he apparently successfully caught) when he collided with the fence surrounding the pole. The City presents no facts that such a focus was either unforeseeable or lacking in due care and the nature of the sport suggests otherwise. The extensive discussion in Luna regarding a property owner's placement of items which may be encountered by a sports participant is instructive on this point as well and shows that the City's obvious argument does not entitle it to summary judgment because there are no facts showing that the pole and fence were or should have been obvious to Mr. Kopp while he was playing that day at Crocker Amazon Park. (part 2 of 3)=(302/HK)

Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. The Defendant's request for monetary sanctions is severed and a hearing on whether sanctions should be awarded is continued to August 21, 2018. rulings.Case Title: IMMER JAIR BENITEZ PALENCIA VS. DANIEL Y-T LAU ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, July 24, 2018, Line 5, CROSS DEFENDANT EDWARD RODRIGUEZ MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Cross-defendant Edward Rodriguez's motion for summary judgment as to the cross-complaints filed by Daniel Lau, Lydia Hwang and Wilfredo Alfaro is denied. Mr. Rodriguez fails to maintain his initial summary judgment burden and, even if he did, there is a triable dispute whether he is liable as an employer of Mr. Alfaro and/or plaintiff Imer Palencia. "When an employee of a contractor is injured, and the contractor is unlicensed and uninsured at the time of injury, the injured employee's recourse may be against not only the contractor, but also against the landowner who hired the contractor, as an additional employer." (Vebr v. Culp (2015) 241 Cal.App.4th 1044, 1051-1052.) Mr. Rodriguez has failed to present sufficient facts to show that he is not liable as an employer. Moreover, the undisputed evidence shows that Mr. Rodriguez agreed to pay for half of the cost of the tree-trimming work. (Rodriguez Dec., par. 10.) There is a triable issue whether Mr. Rodriguez was acting as the employer of Mr. Alfaro and/or Mr. Palencia. The fact that Mr. Yang selected Mr. Alfaro and was the only person who communicated with him is not dispositive. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for cross-complainants is required to prepare a proposed order in compliance with CCP 437c(g) which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: GILBERT PAPAZIAN II VS. KEVIN MICHAEL SULLIVAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday July 24, 2018, Line 5, Motion For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: No opposition or response was filed in connection with the Court's Order to Show Case directed to attorney Andrew Dimitriou dated July 2, 2018. Sanctions in the sum of $1500.00 are imposed on said attorney referable to his failure to file pleadings supporting the motion for reconsideration. While the motion was withdrawn prior to the hearing, the Plaintiff filed formal opposition to the motion and sanctions are warranted. The monetary sanction shall be paid to plaintiff, Gilbert Papazian, within 20 days of service of a copy of this Order with Notice of Entry. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTOPHER KENDRICK ET AL VS. CSE SAFEGAURD INSURANCE COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, July 24, 2018, Line 9, DEFENDANT CSE SAFEGAURD INSURANCE COMPANY's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant CSE Safeguard Insurance Company's demurrer to both causes of action in the complaint filed by plaintiffs Christopher Kendrick and Gamin Circe is sustained with 20 days leave to amend as to both causes of action. Plaintiffs are given leave to amend to plead more ultimate facts to support their claims for breach of contract and bad faith. (Construction Protective Services, Inc. v. TIG Specialty Ins. Co. (2002) 29 Cal.4th 189, 198-199; Croskey, Cal. Practice Guide: Insurance Litigation (The Rutter Group 2017) par. 15:56.) The complaint does not adequately plead a breach of contract claim. The complaint does not describe the policy period or when or how the loss occurred, and only generally describes the terms of the contract. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for CSE is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: WENDY MOORE VS. JONES DAY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal **(Redacted Version)**
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, July 24, 2018, Line 11, DEFENDANT JONES DAY's Motion To Seal **(Redacted Version)**. Hearing required. The hearing will be at 1:30pm, not 9:30am. If any counsel who intended to participate in the hearing is unavailable at 1:30pm, counsel should meet and confer and agree on a date for a hearing at 1:30pm at the earliest possible date other than July 26, 2018 and notify the court of the agreed hearing date by email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org. While defendant Jones Day has not explicitly requested that the courtroom be closed for the hearing on the motions to seal, the logic of the positions it takes in its papers strongly suggests that Jones Day believes the courtroom should be closed for the hearing. The logic of the positions taken by plaintiff Wendy Moore strongly suggests that she opposes doing so. To avoid possible prejudice to Jones Day's asserted overriding interests by the holding of a public hearing on the motions before it can be determined whether there is an overriding interest that requires that the courtroom be closed for the hearing on the motions to seal, the court will close the courtroom to the public for the sole purpose of holding a hearing whether or not the courtroom should be closed for the hearing on the two motions to seal. While the merits of the motions to seal are inextricably tied to the threshold issue of whether the courtroom should be closed for hearing on those motions, the closed courtroom hearing will be confined to the threshold issue unless and until a further order is made closing the courtroom for the hearing on the motions to seal. =(302/HK)
